speaker: interviewer
text: '95. You were telling me about some of the entertainment and things in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's also something what nobody actually can understand, how that could happen in these sorts of camps. It was-- what I said already before-- just a normal community, as you find outside. So there was also entertainment. And Jewish people are really good at this. And we had the cream of the cream in Camp Westerbork. And so it came that the German authorities-- how they ever could have agreed to it, I don't know-- but they spent quite a lot of money on it. I know, for instance, that the first show in the camp, they spent 16,000 guilders on it, for the clothing and what have you, and for the show, needed-- 16,000 guilders. And that was, in that particular time, quite a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it is strange [? thing ?] what could happen then, that there was a show on. And a new transport was arriving in the camp. They said, what's on here, eh? People are laughing, there is a show on in the camp. And after the show went out, OK, these people start to be registered, normal procedure in the camp. The only one show we have seen, ourselves, and we were invited by the Jewish people, because Gemmeker was, most of the nights, there to enjoy himself, because what was there else, in the camp? So the show, if he had nothing to do at home or in the office, he went over to see the show. There was a secretary, and there were two nice chairs in front. Who was the secretary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hassel, Frau Hassel. And then they said to me one evening, won't you join us, and see the show yourself? Because Gemmeker is not coming. I said, OK. And they seated us in his two seats, for him and his secretary. Now, well, we saw the whole show, of course. And it was a very beautiful one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of show was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sketches, all sketches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they famous actors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very-- very-- very much so, very famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Max Ehrlich is one of them. From Holland was Ester Phillips, and, well-- to get off hand, so many names. Willy Rosen, Erich Ziegler, you can see, mostly German names they were, also. Because the shows were in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them get dispensation from going on transports, because of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at last moment. All these people left by the last transport on the 1st of September. But up to that time, we had six shows all together. And they had to do all the rehearsals in the camp. I saw some of the rehearsals there. I got to know more of it. And they were, of course, humorous. One of-- the show we saw, for instance-- there was a sketch in it, [NON-ENGLISH]. That was just the sabotage piece-- [NON-ENGLISH] do it slowly. We have got all the time and that, in a song, produced in a song. And the show that [NON-ENGLISH], that's the same, what you have here in the first period of Australia, the [NON-ENGLISH], that idea. Do it long time. So we are not in a hurry. If you are not in a hurry, we certainly aren't, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who attended the shows? Who was the audience?

speaker: interviewee
text: The audience, the people of the camp. And they had free entry. There was no-- no money to pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. The only one I know, if others also joined in, but I don't expect it. Gemmeker was a typical person, in that sense, that well, you were quite quickly named as to be Jewish-friendly. And if one of his soldiers showed that, well, that would have been wrong. So I don't expect that many others of the staff would have been there. I know of certain high officers whom he invited, to show how the camp was going on and how he did it. They were joined in on the shows, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his secretary, Frau Hassel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was bad. That was a nasty girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did most of the bad things to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always trying to find something. And she got her informations, too, and tried to influence Gemmeker on that particular thing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what did she do? Can you tell me one of the incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the incidents is a little thing. Our eldest one was just playing with Jewish children. Oh, why not? Children are children, and didn't she know anything about the difference. Children are children. So she told Gemmeker that our eldest was playing with Jewish children. So we got a message that it shouldn't happen anymore. So we had to tie her up, inside, that she couldn't go to the other children. But tie a child up. So I asked her, I said, could work herself loose. Yes, she did. And then we were-- she had pulled out plants, in the flower bed, or so-- So I was charged 25 guilders for it-- all these horrible things. All sort of things, trying to pin-- give you the sense that you feel that there is someone watching you. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the medical attention like in the camp hospital facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the hospital-- one of the best hospitals in the whole of the country. I mean, you had the best surgeons there. I mean, these were brainy people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Spanier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He treated my wife. He treated me. And I was at all these things, I did. I went to the dentist there. I got my physio there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were all Jews, who treated you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who could go to the hospital? Could anybody be treated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the people of the camps, yes, could be treated. Even Gemmeker was treated by Spanier. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their relationship like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between Gemmeker and Schlesinger, it was very good, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gemmeker and Spanier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I often said, when the question was asked, who is the boss? The actual boss? Then I say, it was Dr. Spanier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think Spanier was even more important than Schlesinger to Gemmeker. Spanier had, at one stage, because there was polio going on in the camp. And Spanier is the only one who had it made possible to postpone transports, for about two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the infectious disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was shortly after the birth of our second one, October, November, she was six weeks old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So that, perhaps, how old she was. And then I had to bring her outside the camp, as well, because with the infectious disease. So she was six weeks out of the camp with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did the atmosphere in the camp change when there were no transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. I mean, then it was quite happy congregation there. Because the-- the pressure was off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when they started again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when it started, oh, we were always hoping that this particular week was the last transport, and that the liberation was near. So you were living, actually, from week to week. And when it was postponed-- that was over the Christmas, '43, '44-- well, that it came absolutely to a standstill, because of the infectious diseases.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had typhus, typhus in the camp at that time, and the bad part was polio. Now, this was one thing what Dr. Spanier did. And hardly anyone who got polio in the camp had any after-effects of it-- so no paralyzation, or whatever. And what did he do? He called in the camp for people who had polio in their child period. And he took blood of them, and treated the new patients with. So there was a certain resistance in it, apparently. And that was the first time that was used that way. So he made a vaccine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of. He couldn't actually develop it, because there were no things in the camps. So it was practically, say, a straightforward transfer, just the same as a blood transfusion, perhaps clean it or something. That's the way he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him, in the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the camp until the end. I was [NON-ENGLISH] with Dr. Spanier because this other thing what happen. There was a Jewish child, baby. They expected it was a Jewish child, but they didn't know it, and they couldn't place it, actually. And so it was from a Jewish mother, outside was under. And, well, treason again, someone, so she was picked up, or-- She was picked up, but not as the mother of the child. But these foster parents, who were looking after the child, they gave me some information, [INAUDIBLE] again. If I could make a picture of the child, because that they wanted to have something of the child. So that's what I did. I made that, in the camp. Because I knew the photographer there, too. And he did it, automatically.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my wife brought the photos outside, and he wanted to take care of bringing it to that family, again. But he was arrested at the particular moment. And they found that picture on his body. And they said, who's the child? It must be Jewish child. And they were interrogating all night on him, for that particular one. And that's another long story, of course. He was there for about six weeks, arrested. And that child was called after him, Fritz. He was-- his Christian name, the first name was Fritz, Fritz Spanier. And because they didn't know anything about the child, so he was registered as Fritz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how did I get to know that picture never reached them, at that particular moment, anyhow. Because that was of the arrest of the other person, which was my contact person through the underground. I called him [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] was his wife. His real name was [? Stoker ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was in Assen at the 6th of December. I never forget that date. And suddenly, I saw him coming out, his arm in the sling, and said, hey. Where do you come from? He said, I'm just released from prison. Come over, and we are going to my shop. He had a grocery store. And we sat for about half an hour, 3/4 of an hour or so. And then we went outside, walked further along the street where he wanted to see his brother-in-law that was a butcher. And then suddenly, around the corner, came screaming, squealing, the German-- a state car. And then, bang, bang, bang, on that-- from the-- from the shop. And we stopped and stood, well, about 200 yards, watching it, and said, oh, well. If we would have been in there, that would have been the end of both, hmm? Re-arrested for him, and for me being arrested. So we never found out, actually, at that particular moment. He said to me, farewell. At that time he said, OK, I'm going straight under. Hopefully we see each other after the war. And then after the war, he came to me in the camp. And I gave him the delivery of the deliveries in the camp, after that time, again. And he said, I can't enter the shop at the moment. Could you ask for a release of the key by the Dutch commander in Assen? So I applied for it, and we went over to the shop. But the Germans had taken everything out of the shop, everything. There was nothing left. And it was small-- a small shop. In the front was a shop. There was a room, after it, where you could sit down and watch if people were coming in. And next to it was a kitchen. And in the floor-- the concrete floor was scuffing up, and container-- and cement container for rain water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And alongside there were planks, where they put pots and pans on. And on top of that, there was paper. And we lifted the paper up. And was everything, what could have been very bad for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had there the papers of the-- the letterheads from the lager, Westerbork, camp at Westerbork. We had the stamps there from the signature of Gemmeker. We had a stamp of the [NON-ENGLISH]. Were all stamps what-- the more stamps you had on a letter, the better, more impressive it was. So we had all those things for to be made, in forging letters for release, letters for application, you name it. And we could forge them. And the papers were, really, just left underneath it. They had wiped off the pots and pans, just that way. They would have found it. And in that water tank, they were hanging the stamps on pieces of rope. The rope was standing outside. They never-- never touched it. So that happened. But Fritz, we wanted to get Fritz out at one stage, before this all happened. Get me a Fisherman's. And there was Mrs. [? Stoker ?] There was a bicycle-- and a child seat, at the back, to get him out of the camp. And I wanted to pick up the child, then, and bring it over to-- thank you-- to the-- to the gate. And from there on, she could bring it outside. And that was refused-- refused by Spanier. He said, if you are going to take the camp-- the child out of the camp, then I'm going to straightaway to-- to Gemmeker and tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had had to tell her, Mrs. [? Stoker ?] that there was nothing left for me than to leave the child behind. So that was the power of Spanier. Spanier could do anything. And [? Stoker ?] was your underground contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my contact person to the outside, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was-- he was-- he was the forger of all the documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was one of the-- well, also of my group, the underground. We had-- they had 20 people, all together-- our group. Six of them were executed in Camp Westerbork on an afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they found?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these people had dug themselves in, in between us and around in outside there, in all of the forest, there. And that was also made known to the Germans, by one. They were picked up, a couple of days [INAUDIBLE]. And that happened in an afternoon, also in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got to know it. From underground, I have seen it-- from 24, I think, it was one morning. We were doing all sorts of things. We were advance preparing for a hold up, actually, in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A what, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sort of hold up, and to-- to get quite a number of people out-- 14, 20, I don't know. For that reason, I give a plan of the camp to the people who were organizing it, to do it. And it was in the Hague, discussed in the Hague. And it was also treason, then. The Germans also were discussing it. The Germans came in. And the one from our group who was there, that was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Arthur Brensman. This his secretary. They were there, and they had the plans in front of him. Now, as soon as they heard that the Germans were moving in, she went to the toilet and took all the papers-- tear them up and got them through the toilet, flushed them through. So they couldn't find anything, at all. But he was arrested, and she was arrested, both. And out of that, he never returned. I mean he went over to another camp. She was liberated-- she was released after a couple of months. But she died by the liberation of [? Frohnau ?]-- got a heart attack that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your hold up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the hold up, that was one-- one particular morning. And I heard shots. I said, gee, they're coming, and they're doing it now. So I went to the front, to the window to look what was actually happening. And under stood the German, just in front of the window, so-- I went under, and went upstairs again. And there from there on, they couldn't see me, because there was only small window, and a curtain in front. And then I saw-- the crematorium was practically, then, just practically opposite me. That was for people who just normally died. They didn't bury them anymore, but at the later states they cremated them, there. And I saw in front of the crematorium, standing quite a number of people. I don't know the number. One was a tall fellow, and one was a sturdy fellow. I remembered that. And there was a German officer who was reading the sentence to them. And they were brought behind the crematorium, and they're shot. And I've seen that happen, yeah, how they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Backwards, to the peloton, hands in the neck, and that's how they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were people watching?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was early in the morning, 6, 7 o'clock. The only thing what you could hear was, of course, the shots. But I've seen it, yeah. I made, of course, straight away known to the group, who these were. Afterwards I collected the ashes, put them in 14 urns, you name them urns. And later in 1949 buried. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah, saw quite a number of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back to daily life in the camp, were the-- were the Jews religious? Did you see people praying or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They had a synagogue. They married the people-- there is, on another film I have, is you can hear, even, that they were married according to Jewish rites, under the chuppah. Even the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they followed the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They could do so. Yeah, but of course, they couldn't keep the Sabbath in the same way as they could do outside, because they had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was no problem from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They had their own rabbis. Yeah. They have their own schools. There are the shiva. Yeshiva? Yeshiva. Religious schools-- no, there was-- their own school and everything went on, just as normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Children up to the age of, I think, 13 or 14 had to go to school. And from there on, they had to join the workforce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people help each other, the different ethnic groups in the camp, other than the Dutch and Germans, the religious Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a separation. Every group did their own thing. That was the strife between the groups, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect their lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, how effected-- sometimes badly, consequence transport. And that's what-- if there was a certain strife got enough to Gemmeker. Then Gemmeker said, OK, all together, go. That was the consequence, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When people left on the transports, did they know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first instance, not. But later on, the Germans became quite open with it. Even on the train, there was a sign-- Auschwitz Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody know what was happening in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I had, at one stage, the intention-- because I wanted to know what was going on, on the other side-- so I said to my wife, I said, OK. I can get hold of a German uniform. And I'm going to go in with them, and see what is going on over there. She said, oh, no. You are not. No, you are doing enough. So that's where I didn't go on with it. But then we said to everyone we knew, and who was in the train, OK. Try to hide, at certain spots what we made known to them. Letters in which we get to know, anyhow, what goes on in Auschwitz. Well, the message-- we got letters back, but not any further than the arrival of the train. In those letters it was always said, we see high chimneys. It must be an industrial area. And now we have to get out of the train. And that was the end. Never anything more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the letters? Where did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hidden in between the planks of the cattle wagons, in the floor boards, sometimes. But we always checked. The same train came back, that we knew. So what the only thing what I got out of the train was the pans. All the rest, we had to clean them out, ourselves, in the camp's work. So that's where you got to find every corner, where there was something hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the trains dirty when they came back to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much. This was at-- the only thing, what the Germans got out was the-- what was left over, say, in the train itself. And the pans for the toilets were there, the pan for toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened towards the end of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of 1944, ah, well, we got in-- the change started. We had in September '44, the third and the fourth September day, practically emptied the camp. And that said, oh, well. Now it's really bad. Everyone had to go. And that's where most of the German, the older people of the camp-- what they called [NON-ENGLISH], or so name for it-- they had to go, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had, at that time, about 300, 400 people left, from a population of about 3,000, 4,000. In two transports, they moved them out. And that's why the camp-- well, it was absolutely silence, practically, in the camp at that time-- says, yeah, we lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then on the 17th of September, and that's what something you may have heard about, that was the Battle of Arnhem. And there is a film about it, That's where we expect it, now. And now, they are coming across the river Rhine. Now it doesn't take only a couple of days, and then we are all liberated. And that went totally wrong. And so they had to move back towards Nijmegen. That's on the other side of the river, Rhine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took us then further nine months before-- September, maybe seven months-- before we actually got liberated, ourselves. But in that time, the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH], the Dutch socialists, got the fright of their life. And they moved over to Camp Westerbork. And we had about 8,000 of them. 8,000. And they came the same way, also packed with their belongings, what they could carry. And that's where we had our laugh, and said, yeah. Now you go, eh? That's good for us. And a couple of days before, before that happened, the Germans were killing off quite some cattle on the-- on the farm. They killed some cows and a couple of-- quite some cows, and pigs. And I went over to the kitchen, to see what was going on there. Now, well, there was a German standing-- a German officer. And said, what are you doing here? Said, well, check up, my normal job. And he said, yeah, we know what you are doing here. And he showed me, in his hand, he took out of his pocket and showed the bullets and said, as soon as we have found out what you really are, then I'm the one who's going to put these bullets into your body. That was happening a couple of days before. And then the-- We'll break for the end of the tape.
